title: cicuta maculata var. bolanderi
intro: Cicuta maculata var. bolanderi, commonly known as Western Water Hemlock, is a highly toxic plant that belongs to the carrot family (Apiaceae). While it has no significant health benefits due to its extreme toxicity, it has been used in traditional medicine for its therapeutic actions, which include anthelmintic, antispasmodic, and expectorant properties. The bioactive constituents of Western Water Hemlock include alkaloids, glycosides, and terpenoids, which contribute to its toxic effects. However, it's essential to note that the plant is highly poisonous and should be handled with extreme caution, if at all.
side_effects: Cicuta maculata var. bolanderi can have side effects if used improperly, such as inducing seizures, muscle spasms, and respiratory failure due to its high concentration of cicutoxin. Ingestion of the plant's toxic compounds can also cause vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal pain, often leading to dehydration and electrolyte imbalances. In severe cases, improper use of the plant can result in cardiac arrest, coma, and even death. Additionally, exposure to cicutoxin can cause long-term neurological damage, including tremors, numbness, and cognitive impairment.
